title: 第 33 節
en: I say once more, there is no harm in all these ethnological combinations. Let us by all means consult the lower forms of religion and mythology, as possibly they may offer analogies to some features in the Vedic religion; but to call them primitive, and to represent what we see among the savages of the nineteenth century as the underlying stratum of the Veda, is surely no more than the expression of a fond hope, not of a fact established by scientific evidence. The danger here as elsewhere lies in excessive generalisation. Even the so-called stone age, which is often postulated as the universal precursor of a more advanced civilisation, is far from being universal, and exhibits totally different features when we find it, either on stratified or unstratified soil. It is the same with what may be called the stone age of the human intellect. In both cases we must learn to distinguish.
Evil Spirits.

There is no difficulty in tracing a belief in evil, unclean, and maleficent spirits, such as abound in the Atharva-veda, to the same soil which produced a faith in good and beneficent spirits. We need P 2 212 EVIL SPIRITS. [chap.

not go for them to the aboriginal inhabitants of India, or to the Blacks of Australia. Some of the great Vedic gods like Rudra and the Maruts often assume a double aspect. They are unkind as well as kind, they cause diseases though they likewise heal them. If Agni, fire, is constantly invoked as pavaka, as the purifying god, and if fire l was used in many places as a real or imaginary purifier for cattle and men, as destroying pestilence and every kind of disease, if it is used so even now in many parts of the world, the hostile powers which it was meant to destroy, might well assume the same personal or mythological character as Agni himself who, as the devourer of raw flesh, often assumes most hideous forms. We have plenty of evil spirits in the Veda, such as Vrrtra, and, in the plural, Vntras, Rakshas's, Yatudhanas, Pisa/.as, &c. Of course nothing is easier than to say that they were borrowed from the native races of India, but this, which was formerly a very favourite expedient, would hardly commend itself now to any serious scholar 2 excepting always the cases where Dravidian words can actually be discovered in Sanskrit. The Dasyus themselves or the black natives of India 3, were represented as so hideous that they might have served perfectly well as the models of devils, without our having to call in the actual devils whom they are supposed to have worshipped and to have made over to the Aryas +.
1 Physical Eeligion, p. 284, 'Purification by Fire.'
2 Cf. Kittel, a Tract on Sacrifice, p. 1 5. s M. M., Letter to Bunsen, p. 83 seq.
4 Atharva-veda XVIII, 2, 28.
Il] THE FITRIS OR ANCESTRAL SPIRITS. 213 The Pitris or Ancestral Spirits, Pretas.

If the PitWs, the souls of the departed, were invoked for blessings, it is almost implied that the same spirits could also withhold such blessings or inflict punishment on those who had incurred their displeasure \ Children, who know nothing of ancestral or other spirits, are frightened by the mere remembrance of those whom they have seen dying or in their shrouds. These are really the Pretas, the departed, looked upon as corpses, skeletons, or ghosts, very different from the departed, when conceived and worshipped as PitWs, fathers. Nervous people see visions at all times without requiring any authority from antecedent or prehistoric ages.

However, I say once more, let us never shut our eyes against new evidence, if it can help us to separate the multifarious ingredients of mythology ; let us get as many parallels as we can, only let us be careful to get trustworthy evidence, and let us not change mere parallels into antecedents. If there are gods in the Veda who have no physical antecedents, let us explain them in any way we can, but if a physical type is still visible, however dimly, it ought always to be considered first.
Aryan Mythology explained by a Comparison with Semitic Mythology.
We have now to see what light we may really hope to gain from a study of the religions and mythologies of non- Aryan people, without confining 1 Cf. Rv. X, 15, 6.
214 ARYAN AND SEMITIC MYTHOLOGY. [chap.

our attention to mere savages, for a better appreciation of certain features of the religion and mythology of the Veda. Some of us may still remember the time when it was the fashion to explain Greek mythology, not indeed by the light of Kafir folklore, but by a comparison with the folklore contained in the Old Testament 1.

A very learned Greek scholar, F. A. Paley, declared that 'it was impossible to doubt that in the garden of the Hesperides we have a tradition of the Garden of Eden, the golden apples guarded by a dragon being the apples which the serpent tempted Eve to gather, or the garden kept by an angel with a flaming sword.' Mr. Gladstone seemed to favour similar ideas, ideas which at the time of Bochart and Huet were looked upon as beyond the reach of reasonable doubt 2. Even now a Journal is published in France, Revue d'Exegese Mythologique, in which there are learned articles on such subjects as ' Les Mythes d Apollon et de Diana, expliques d'apres la Bible.' It may be called mere prejudice to object to all comparison between Semitic and Aryan mythology and folklore. If there are traces of undifferentiated humanity to be found among the Kafirs and Veddahs which lend themselves to comparisons with Vedic folklore, why should not the Semitic nations also have preserved something of that common heirloom ? If all nations were once unmitigated savages, why should nothing of what we call Semitic date from that antediluvian period of universal humanity or universal savagery ? The late Professor Robertson Smith has tried to point 1 Science of Language, ii, p. 510. 2 L. c, p. 505.

Il] AEYAN AND SEMITIC MYTHOLOGY. 21 5 out Semitic customs that seem to admit of comparison with the customs of non-Semitic savages, and though some of our best Semitic scholars have been slow in accepting these identifications, still, unless we suppose that the Semitic character was so strong that it transformed and absorbed all antecedent elements, there is nothing to be said against the principle which he defended. It will be useful, however, to search not only in the Old Testament, but likewise in the Talmudic literature, nay in Phenician and Babylonian folklore, for traces of ante-Semitic customs and superstitions which might lend themselves for comparison with Aryan mythology. Thus, if it proved difficult to discover traces of Animism in the Old Testament, it has been rightly pointed out that Animism is rampant in an ancient hymn ascribed by St. Augustine to the Jewish Church, the Benedicite, which still forms part of our own Church service. This shows, at all events, the wonderful continuity of human thought among Semitic as well as among Aryan nations.

No doubt, the results of comparisons between Aryan and Semitic mythology have hitherto been small and disappointing. It is often said that what remains of these labours is not much more than the recognition of the original solar character of Samson, and even in this case it is Aryan mythology rather that has thrown light on Hebrew mythology, than vice versa. As to other comparisons, such as that of Yama and Yami with Adam and Eve, though tempting at first sight, I shall have to explain afterwards why it can no longer be considered tenable. Some other comparisons between Greek and Semitic names of gods and heroes lately put forward with 2l6 ARYAN AND SEMITIC MYTHOLOGY. [chap.

wonderful sang froid, really take away one's breath, when one remembers how carefully comparisons between Greek and Sanskrit names have been worked out, and have yet failed to satisfy the conscience of many a classical scholar.

It is curious to see how the same scholars who express the gravest doubts as to the phonetic similarity of names such as Varuwa and Ouranos, are satisfied with the vaguest similarities when they compare Semitic and Aryan names, without even attempting anything like a scientific etymological analysis.

M. Victor Barard, for instance, in his Essai de methode en mythologie Grecque, after trying to show that certain Arkadian gods were borrowed from Semitic sources, throws out the hint : ' Presque tout l'Olympe grec est peut-etre d'origine semitique.' This is a bold peut-6tre, particularly when we examine the fragmentary evidence adduced in support of it. Astynorne, we are told, may stand for the Semitic Ast Naama, Orchomenos and Erigone for Erek Hagim, Chalcis for Kart, Pelasgos for Peleg, and Aphrodite for Ashtoret. We are told that machanah is the Phenician name for camp, and that therefore Mvktjvou, Mtjkcovt], Mvkojvos, Miywviov, &c, must all be accepted as corruptions of this Phenician machanah. It is impossible to refute such assertions, because there is really no evidence to lay hold of and to examine. We have guesses and assertions and nothing else. In the case of Aphrodite it has never been denied that her later character was influenced by the worship paid to and the legends told of the Semitic goddess of love. But few Greek scholars could be persuaded that the name of Aphro- II J ARYAN AND SEMITIC MYTHOLOGY. 217 dite was a Greek failure to pronounce Ashtoret, and that the Greeks had no goddess of beauty and love, no Greek Charis, no Aphrogeneia, no Anadyomene, no Enalia, no Ourania, no Aglaia, before they heard of the Kypris, the Kythereia, the Paphla, or the Pandemos, and were induced to recognise in the misshapen statues of Ishtar, and in her licentious worship, something akin to their old goddess Charis, the daughter of Zeus and Harmonia. How Semitic scholars would rejoice if they could produce at least one equation such as Aphrodite = Sk. Abhrad-ita, i. e. come forth from the cloud ; yet no Sanskrit scholar would even listen now to such a comparison.
Dionysos and Semele.

So long as there seemed to be some ground for supposing that the Aryan words for wine were derived from a Semitic language, there was some excuse for looking to a Semitic language for an explanation of the name of Dionysos or his mother Semele. But now that the evidence points clearly to an Aryan origin of olvos and vinum, even that excuse is gone. We are told, nevertheless, that because a Phenician inscription has been found in a bay to the West of the Piraeos \ containing the name Pen 'Samlath, the face of 'Samlath, therefore Semele, body and soul, is a corruption of the Phenician 'Samlath. How 'Samlath became Semele is hardly asked. No doubt it is true that words borrowed from foreign languages are liable to very capricious changes. But there is method even in 1 Hibbert Lectures, Snyce, p. 54.
2l8 DIONYSOS AND SEMELE. [chap.

caprice, as has been shown by Weisse, Saalfeld, and others, in treating of Greek words imported ready made into Latin. Unless we follow the old principle of principiis obsta, we shall soon drop back into the days when Jovis was derived from Jehovah. A Babylonian origin has already been assigned to the name of Dionysos. Mr. Fox Talbot declared that the name of the Sun in Assyrian theology was Daian-nisi or Dian-nisi, ' the judge of men,' and Mr. Robert Brown, jun.1, thought he could discover in this the original name of Dionysos. This conjecture, however, is no longer accepted even by Cuneiform scholars ; no scholar now, I believe, approves of it.

The same spirit has shown itself lately in a comparison between Aryan mythological names occurring in the Lycian inscriptions. Here not even the grammatical character of the inscriptions has as yet been settled, and yet Canon Bawlinson was bold enough to see in the word Lada, occurring at the beginning of several Lycian inscriptions, a word akin to the English Lady, i. e. hlafdige. Nay of late we have been told that the same Lada may be connected with Leto or Latona, the mother of Apollon. Le"to is no doubt a very troublesome name, and there are various difficulties which we shall have to examine, and which have made many scholars hesitate to connect it with Lat. latere or Greek \a9 in Xavddvoi ; but what are these difficulties - compared with this 1 Transactions of the Eoyal Soc. of Liter, viii, 297 ; The Great Dionysiak Myth, ii, 209 (1878).
2 See now Edwin W. Fay, Am. Journal of Philology, vol. xvi, p. 4.
Il] DIONYSOS AND SEMELE. 219 Lycian etymology ? Here a strong protest is necessary indeed, if we do not wish to slide back into the school of Bochart.

That Semitic nations, whether Phenicians or Babylonians, or even Jews, have exercised an influence on the outer life of the Greeks, on their arts, particularly the arts of building and writing, their dress, their commercial customs, &c, is clear enough, but their inner life, their familiar language, their home-grown religion and mythology developed uninjured by contact with foreign nations, and retained so sharply marked a national character that casual foreign importations, such as, for instance, Melikertes or Belos tell by the very contrast their barbarian origin.
Uncertain Character of Ethnological Evidence.

If, then, so little real advantage could be derived from a comparison of Aryan with Semitic mythology, as little as of Aryan with Semitic languages, have we any right to expect a richer harvest from a comparison of the Greeks with illiterate races, such as the Kafirs, or Yeddahs, or the Mincoupies ?
zh: 我再次申明，所有這些民族學上的組合都沒有害處。我們當然可以參考較低等形式的宗教與神話，因為它們可能對吠陀宗教中的某些特徵提供類比；但將這些稱為原始的，並將我們在十九世紀的蠻族中所見到的狀況表現為吠陀的基層，這肯定不過是一種美好願望的表達，而非科學證據所確立的事實。危險在這裡與其他地方一樣，在於過度的概括化。即使是所謂的石器時代，經常被視為更高級文明的普遍前驅，也絕非普遍存在，且當我們在層積或非層積的土壤中發現它時，會呈現出完全不同的特徵。人類智識的所謂石器時代也是如此。在兩種情況下，我們都必須學會作出區分。

在許多民族的宗教中，我們發現人們相信存在著邪惡的靈魂或惡魔。這些信念通常源於對自然力量的誤解，或對疾病、死亡與苦難等無法解釋現象的恐懼。原始人試圖將這些不幸歸因於超自然的邪惡力量，而非自然因素。 在雅利安民族的宗教發展中，邪惡的靈魂概念經歷了深刻的轉變。最初，人們可能將任何令人厭惡或危害的力量都人格化為邪惡的存在。然而，隨著宗教思想的進展，這些原始的邪靈信念逐漸演變為更具系統性的道德對立——善惡二元論。 比較神學的研究顯示，不同文化中對邪惡靈魂的描述存在著驚人的相似性，儘管其具體的名稱與屬性或有差異。這種普遍性提示我們，人類心靈對邪惡力量的觀念可能源於共同的心理根源，而非單純的民族傳承。
追溯信仰邪惡、不潔與惡意精靈（如《阿闥婆吠陀》中大量充斥的那些精靈）的起源，並無困難之處；這些精靈的信仰與對善良、仁慈精靈的信仰，乃是源自同一片沃土。我們需要 P 2 212 邪惡精靈。[chap.

不必追溯到印度的原住民或澳洲的黑人。梨俱吠陀中的許多偉大提婆，如魯陀羅（Rudra）和馬魯特神（the Maruts），常常呈現雙重面貌。他們既不友善亦友善，他們既致疾病亦治療疾病。如果阿耆尼（Agni）火神經常被祈禱為淨化神（pavaka），如果火在許多地方被用作真實的或想象的淨化物以淨化牲畜和人類，摧毀瘟疫和各類疾病，如果即使在世界許多地方現在仍然如此使用，那麼那些它本意摧毀的敵對勢力可以很好地呈現出與阿耆尼本身相同的人格化或神話特徵——阿耆尼作為吞食生肉者，常常呈現最為恐怖的形式。我們在吠陀中擁有許多邪惡精靈，如弗栗多羅（Vrrtra），及其複數形式弗栗多羅（Vntras）、羅剎（Rakshas's）、夜叉毘多那（Yatudhanas）、皮薩剌（Pisa/.as）等。當然，沒有比說它們是從印度本地民族借來更容易的事了，但這種方法曾經是極為流行的權宜之計，如今恐怕難以為任何認真的學者所接受²——除非在確實能在梵文中發現達羅毘荼語詞彙的情況下。達薩人（the Dasyus）本身或印度的黑人原住民³被描繪得如此可怖，以至於他們可以完全充當魔鬼的範本，而無須我們去援引他們本應信仰且轉讓給雅利安人⁴的實際魔鬼。
1 《物質宗教》，第284頁，〈火之淨化〉(Purification by Fire)。
2 參見基特爾《論祭祀》，第15頁。3 穆勒致本森信函，第83頁以下。
4 《阿闥婆吠陀》(Atharva-veda) 第十八卷，2，28。
皮特里（Pitris）或祖先靈魂、餓鬼（Pretas）。

如果祖靈（Pit*W*s）——亡者之魂——被祈求降福，這幾乎暗示同樣的神靈也可能拒絕賜予此類祝福，或對那些招致其不悅的人施加懲罰。不知祖靈或其他神靈為何物的孩童，僅憑對他們曾見過垂死或被裹屍布包裹之人的回憶，就會感到恐懼。這些實際上是餓鬼（Pretas）——亡者——被視為屍體、骸骨或幽靈，與被視為祖父們（fathers）而被祭祀的亡靈截然不同。神經敏感之人在任何時刻都會看到幻象，無需援引任何先前或史前時代的權威。
然而，我要再次強調，讓我們永遠不要對新的證據閉上眼睛，如果它能幫助我們分離神話中的眾多成分；讓我們盡可能地蒐集平行現象，只是讓我們謹慎地取得可信的證據，並且不要將純粹的平行現象轉化為先行因素。如果吠陀中有些提婆沒有物質的先行因素，讓我們以任何我們能夠的方式來解釋它們；但若仍然能夠看到某種物質型態的蹤跡，無論多麼微弱，它應當始終被優先考慮。
雅利安神話學：與閃語族神話學的比較詮釋
我們現在需要考察，在研究非雅利安民族的宗教與神話學時，我們究竟能真正期望獲得什麼樣的光照，而不限於此。¹ --- ¹ 參見梨俱吠陀第十卷第15讚歌第6節。
214 雅利安及閃語族神話學 [章節]
我們的注意力轉向單純的野蠻人，以便更好地領會吠陀宗教與神話的某些特徵。我們中有些人也許還記得那個時代，當時人們習慣於解釋希臘神話，雖然不是借助卡菲爾民間故事之光，而是通過與舊約聖經所包含的民間故事進行比較¹。

一位學識淵博的希臘文獻學者F‧A‧佩利（F. A. Paley）曾宣稱，「不容置疑的是，在赫斯珀里得斯的花園中，我們擁有一個關於伊甸園的傳統，由龍守護的金蘋果就是那條蛇誘惑夏娃採摘的蘋果，或者是由一位天使用燃燒的劍把守的花園」。格拉德斯頓先生似乎贊成類似的看法，這些看法在波夏爾特和韋特時代曾被視為超越合理懷疑之外的事物。即使在今日，法國仍出版著一份期刊《神話解釋評論》（*Revue d'Exegese Mythologique*），其中刊載著涉及諸如「阿波羅與戴安娜的神話——根據聖經的解釋」（*Les Mythes d'Apollon et de Diana, expliques d'apres la Bible*）等主題的學術文章。對所有比較閃語族和雅利安神話與民俗的嘗試提出反對，可能會被稱作純粹的偏見。如果在卡菲爾人和韋達族人中能發現未分化的人類遺跡，這些遺跡足以與吠陀民俗相比較，那麼為什麼閃語族民族也不能保存某些共同的遺產呢？如果所有民族曾經都是不折不扣的野蠻人，為什麼被我們稱作閃語族的事物中就沒有任何東西源自那個洪水前的全人類或全面野蠻的時期呢？已故的羅伯遜‧史密斯教授曾試圖指出¹ ¹ 語言科學，第二卷，第510頁。 ² 同上，第505頁。

在閃語族習俗中尋找似乎可與非閃語族野蠻人的習俗相比較的內容，儘管我們最優秀的一些閃語族學者在接受這些認同時態度遲疑，然而除非我們假定閃語族性格是如此強勢，以致它改造並吸收了所有先前的要素，否則對於他所捍衛的原則沒有任何反對的理由。然而，有用之舉在於不僅在舊約全書中搜尋，同樣也在塔木德文獻、甚至腓尼基和巴比倫民俗中搜尋前閃語族習俗與迷信的蹤跡，這些蹤跡可能有助於與雅利安神話相比較。因此，如果在舊約全書中發現萬物有靈論的蹤跡證實為困難，那麼已有人恰當地指出，萬物有靈論在聖‧奧古斯丁所歸於猶太教會的一首古代讚美詩《聖德頌》（Benedicite）中盛行，該讚美詩至今仍是我們教會禮儀的組成部分。這至少表明了人類思想在閃語族及雅利安民族之間的奇妙延續性。

無庸置疑，雅利安與閃語族神話之間的比較研究迄今為止成果甚微，令人失望。人們常言，這些研究工作所遺留下來的，不過是對參孫（Samson）原初太陽神性質的認可，即便在這種情況下，也是雅利安神話對希伯來神話有所啟蒙，而非反之。至於其他比較，譬如亞摩（Yama）與亞彌（Yami）與亞當和夏娃的對比，雖然初看起來頗為引人矚目，我將在後文詳細說明為何此說不復可信。至於近來提出的希臘與閃語族神名及英雄名稱之間的其他若干比較，216 雅利安與閃語族神話 [章節
令人驚嘆的沉著冷靜，確實會令人屏息，當人回想起希臘語與梵語名稱之間的比較是如何細心地推敲出來，卻仍未能滿足許多古典學者的良知時。
有趣的是，某些學者在質疑瓦魯那（Varuwa）與烏拉諾斯（Ouranos）等名稱的語音相似性時態度最為謹慎，但在比較閃語族與雅利安名稱時，卻對最模糊的相似性感到滿足，甚至未嘗試進行任何類似科學的語源分析。

以維克多‧巴拉爾（M. Victor Barard）為例，在其《希臘神話方法論試論》（Essai de methode en mythologie Grecque）中，於試圖證明某些阿卡迪亞神祇源自閃語族來源之後，拋出了這樣的暗示：「幾乎整個希臘奧林帕斯山可能是閃語族起源的。」這是一個大膽的「可能」，特別是當我們審視為此提出的零碎證據時。據稱，阿斯圖諾墨（Astynorne）可代表閃語族的 Ast Naama，俄耳刻歐墨諾斯（Orchomenos）與厄里戈涅（Erigone）可代表 Erek Hagim，卡爾基斯（Chalcis）可代表 Kart，珀拉斯戈斯（Pelasgos）可代表 Peleg，阿佛洛狄忒（Aphrodite）可代表 Ashtoret。據稱 machanah 是腓尼基人對營地的稱呼，因此 Mvktjvou、Mtjkcovt]、Mvkojvos、Miywviov 等等，必然都應被視為對這個腓尼基詞 machanah 的訛變。對於這類主張是不可能進行反駁的，因為實在沒有真正的證據可供握持與檢視。我們只有猜測與斷言，別無其他。就阿佛洛狄忒而言，從未有人否認她後來的性格受到了對閃語族愛神的崇拜與傳說的影響。但少有希臘學者會被說服，認為阿佛洛狄忒這一名字是希臘人對 Ashtoret 的發音失誤，以及希臘人在聽聞 Kypris、Kythereia、Paphla 或 Pandemos 之前，並無美與愛的女神、無希臘的卡里斯（Charis）、無阿佛洛革涅亞（Aphrogeneia）、無阿那迪歐墨涅（Anadyomene）、無厄那利亞（Enalia）、無烏剌尼亞（Ourania）、無阿格萊亞（Aglaia），以及隨後才被誘導在依什塔爾（Ishtar）那變形的塑像以及其放蕩的崇拜中，認出某種與其古老女神卡里斯——宙斯與哈爾莫尼亞之女——相近的東西。若閃語族學者至少能提出一個等式，如阿佛洛狄忒 = 梵文 Abhrad-ita，亦即「從雲中顯現」，他們將會欣喜若狂；然而，現在沒有任何梵文學者甚至願意傾聽這樣的比較。
狄俄尼索斯與塞墨勒

只要似乎還有某些根據可以假定雅利安人關於葡萄酒的詞彙源自閃語族語言，就還有某種藉口去在閃語族語言中尋求狄俄尼索斯（Dionysos）或其母親塞墨勒（Semele）之名的解釋。但既然證據現已清楚指向 olvos 和 vinum 的雅利安起源，連那個藉口也就消失了。然而我們卻被告知，因為在比雷埃夫斯灣西邊發現了一份腓尼基銘文，其中含有「潘·薩姆拉特」（Pen 'Samlath）之名，「薩姆拉特的面容」（the face of 'Samlath），所以塞墨勒這個詞在身體和靈魂上都是對腓尼基語「薩姆拉特」（'Samlath）的訛變。至於「薩姆拉特」如何變成塞墨勒，幾乎沒人提出質疑。毫無疑問，從外來語言借用的詞彙確實容易發生非常反覆無常的變化。但即使在這種變化中也存在著方法¹ ¹ *Hibbert Lectures, Snyce*, p. 54.
218 狄俄尼索斯與塞墨勒 [章節]

如韋塞（Weisse）、薩爾費爾德（Saalfeld）及其他學者在論述希臘文詞彙如何整體移入拉丁文時所證實的，這種武斷之舉不勝枚舉。除非我們遵循「防患於未然」（principiis obsta）的古老原則，否則我們將很快倒退回將朱庇特（Jovis）溯源於耶和華（Jehovah）的年代。巴比倫人的起源已被指派給狄俄尼索斯（Dionysos）的名號。福克斯‧塔爾博特先生曾宣稱，亞述神學中太陽的名稱是達揚‧尼西（Daian-nisi）或迪安‧尼西（Dian-nisi），意為「人類的審判者」，小羅伯特‧布朗先生則認為他能在此發現狄俄尼索斯原始名號的蹤跡。然而，這一推測即使在楔形文字學者中也不再被接受；我相信現今沒有任何學者贊同它。

同樣的精神最近也表現在對呂基亞銘文中出現的雅利安神話名字的比較上。在這裡，銘文的語法特徵甚至還未得到確立，然而巴林森教士卻大膽地在出現於幾處呂基亞銘文開頭的詞彙 Lada 中，看到了與英文 Lady（即 hlafdige）相近的詞。不僅如此，最近我們還被告知同一個 Lada 可能與 Leto 或 Latona（阿波羅之母）相關。Leto 無疑是個相當棘手的名字，存在著各種困難，我們將需要加以檢視，而這些困難已經使許多學者在將其與拉丁文 latere 或希臘文 λανθάνω 相連結時有所遲疑；但相比於此，這些困難又算得了什麼呢？
見 Edwin W. Fay 著，《美國語言學期刊》（*Am. Journal of Philology*），第十六卷，第四頁。
219 呂基亞語源學？若我們不願滑回博夏特學派，此處誠有必要提出強烈異議。

那些閃語族民族，無論是腓尼基人、巴比倫人，甚或猶太人，確實對希臘人的外在生活造成了影響，涉及他們的藝術，特別是建築與書寫的技藝、服飾、商業習俗等等，這一點不言而喻。然而，他們的內在生活、他們的日常語言、他們土生土長的宗教與神話卻在與外來民族的接觸中完整地發展，並保有如此鮮明的民族特性，以至於那些零星的外來傳入物，例如梅利凱爾特斯（Melikertes）或貝洛斯（Belos）等，反而透過強烈的對比暴露了它們的蠻族來源。
民族學證據的不確定性
如果說，從雅利安神話與閃語族神話的比較中所獲得的實際益處如此之少，就如同從雅利安語言與閃語族語言的比較中所獲得的益處一樣微薄，那麼我們有什麼理由期待從希臘人與文化水準較低的民族（如卡菲爾人、耶達人或敏庫皮人）的比較中獲得更豐碩的成果呢？
